title: Invasive Species Impact Network
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Introduce invasive species into a native food web and watch cascade effects ripple through the ecosystem over simulated time steps.

learningObjectives: Assess how the introduction of an invasive species cascades through a native food web
Trace direct and indirect effects of invasive species on multiple trophic levels
Compare different types of invasive species and their distinct cascade patterns
Evaluate whether ecosystem recovery is possible after invasive species removal
prerequisites: Understanding of food webs and trophic levels
Familiarity with the concept of invasive species

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Scenario comparison with cascading effects observation
keyQuestions: Why do invasive species often have advantages over native species in a new ecosystem?
How do indirect effects of invasive species differ from direct effects?
Can an ecosystem fully recover after an invasive species is removed?
Why is prevention more effective than removal for managing invasive species?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think invasive species only affect the species they directly eat or compete with, missing indirect cascade effects
Many students believe that removing an invasive species will automatically restore the ecosystem to its original state
Students may think all non-native species are invasive, when many introduced species do not become harmful
assessmentOpportunities: Evaluate student cascade pathway diagrams for completeness of direct and indirect effects
Compare student scenario comparison tables across the three invasive species
Assess written explanations of why ecosystem recovery is often incomplete after invasive removal
